meta.title: COVID-19 Occupational Exposure & Morbidity — Concept Lattice (Rewritten)
meta.subtitle: A fully rewritten knowledge graph mapping pandemic challenges from general ethical principles down to specific occupational dilemmas and outcomes.
meta.domainName: COVID-19 epidemiology, occupation, mitigations, and morbidity risk across social strata
meta.searchPlaceholder: ethics, equity, reciprocity, precarious work, ventilation, respirators, variants, superspreading, sick pay, care homes, schools, long COVID…
meta.userContextPlaceholder: e.g., 'care home worker in shared housing', 'teacher with poor ventilation', 'gig driver without sick pay'…
meta.initialPrompt: Explore how ethical principles and social stratification shape occupational exposure, mitigations, and morbidity risks during COVID-19. Click concepts to trace from general principles to concrete dilemmas.
meta.promptTemplate: Given the user's context: {{userContext}}, explain the most relevant pathway from principles → structures → epidemiology → governance → occupation → mitigations → outcomes. Provide practical questions to ask and the main trade-offs.

levels.title: Ethics, rights & legitimacy (most general)
levels.hint: Foundational principles that constrain all decisions: dignity, justice, reciprocity, transparency, proportionality, trust.

levels.title: Social structure & stratification
levels.hint: How power, resources, and social position shape exposure, options, and downstream harm (income, housing, discrimination, job security).

levels.title: Epidemiological system & uncertainty
levels.hint: Transmission dynamics, variants, measurement limits, and decision-making under uncertainty (prevalence, R, aerosols, superspreading).

levels.title: Institutions, policy & governance
levels.hint: Public health, labour regulation, organisational duty of care, and accountability mechanisms that translate principles into rules.

levels.title: Occupations, settings & constraints
levels.hint: Sectors, roles, and work realities: task mix, contact patterns, physical environments, and ability to adopt controls.

levels.title: Exposure pathways & mitigations (operational)
levels.hint: Mechanisms of exposure and layered controls (engineering/admin/PPE/testing/vaccination), including implementation quality.

levels.title: Specific outcomes, dilemmas & cases (most specific)
levels.hint: Concrete consequences and real-world trade-offs: infection/morbidity, long COVID, moral injury, and case-level decision points.

nodes.label: Ethical foundations in a pandemic
nodes.summary: Core ethical commitments that guide and limit action when lives, livelihoods, and liberties conflict.
nodes.bodyHtml:
A pandemic creates unavoidable trade-offs. Ethical principles set the outer boundaries of what is acceptable, and help justify burdens placed on different groups.

Key idea: decisions are not only technical; they must be legitimate to those who bear the risks.

nodes.label: Dignity & non‑maleficence
nodes.summary: Avoid preventable harm; treat workers and patients as ends in themselves, not expendable inputs.
nodes.bodyHtml: Even under emergency, workers are not 'disposable'. Policies should reduce avoidable exposure and avoid shifting risk onto those with least power.

nodes.label: Justice & equity
nodes.summary: Fair distribution of risk, protection, and care—especially for those already disadvantaged.
nodes.bodyHtml: Equity asks: who is more exposed, who is protected, and who pays the price when protections fail?

nodes.label: Reciprocity for essential workers
nodes.summary: If society requires some people to face higher risk, it owes them stronger protections and supports.
nodes.bodyHtml: Reciprocity motivates priority access to PPE, vaccination, sick pay, compensation, and mental health support for frontline roles.

nodes.label: Proportionality & least‑restrictive means
nodes.summary: Interventions should match the level of risk and use the least restrictive effective option.
nodes.bodyHtml: Restrictions on movement or work should be evidence-informed, time-limited, and revisable as conditions change.

nodes.label: Transparency & explainability
nodes.summary: People comply more when they understand why decisions are made and what evidence is used.
nodes.bodyHtml: Explain assumptions, uncertainty, and trade-offs. Hidden rationing or inconsistent rules erode trust.

nodes.label: Solidarity & collective responsibility
nodes.summary: Infection risk is shared; individual choices affect others, especially in interdependent workplaces.
nodes.bodyHtml: Solidarity supports mutual obligations: employers to protect, workers to follow controls, government to resource systems.

nodes.label: Rights: safety, privacy, and non-discrimination
nodes.summary: Balancing workplace safety with privacy (testing data) and protection from discrimination.
nodes.bodyHtml: Testing and contact tracing can reduce risk but can also stigmatise or penalise workers without safeguards.

nodes.label: Social gradient of risk
nodes.summary: Exposure and harm follow social gradients: lower income and marginalised groups often face higher risk and fewer buffers.
nodes.bodyHtml: Risk is not evenly distributed. Social position shapes where you work, how you travel, where you live, and access to healthcare.

nodes.label: Precarious work & bargaining power
nodes.summary: Insecure contracts, gig work, and probation periods reduce the ability to refuse unsafe work or take leave.
nodes.bodyHtml: When missing a shift means missing rent, 'choice' to isolate is constrained. Protections must account for these realities.

nodes.label: Housing crowding & household transmission
nodes.summary: Crowded or multi-generational housing increases within-household spread and makes isolation difficult.
nodes.bodyHtml: Workplace exposure can translate into household outbreaks, amplifying harm in already crowded living situations.

nodes.label: Discrimination & structural racism
nodes.summary: Disparities in job types, healthcare access, and treatment lead to unequal exposure and outcomes.
nodes.bodyHtml: Discrimination can affect assignment to higher-risk tasks, ability to raise concerns, and quality of healthcare received.

nodes.label: Caregiving burden
nodes.summary: Unpaid care responsibilities (children, elders) affect exposure networks and economic vulnerability during isolation.
nodes.bodyHtml: School closures and illness increase care needs; this can force job loss or risky attendance when alternatives are limited.

nodes.label: Digital divide & remote work inequality
nodes.summary: Ability to work remotely is stratified by occupation, education, and income.
nodes.bodyHtml: Remote work reduces exposure for some while concentrating in-person risk among others.

nodes.label: Dependence on public transport
nodes.summary: Commuting constraints can add exposure for workers who cannot drive or shift schedules.
nodes.bodyHtml: Crowded buses/trains add contacts outside the workplace and can compound risk for lower-paid essential roles.

nodes.label: Transmission pathways (airborne, droplets, contact)
nodes.summary: Understanding how SARS‑CoV‑2 spreads is essential for matching controls to mechanisms.
nodes.bodyHtml: Airborne transmission dominates in many indoor settings; close-range droplets and contaminated hands can contribute in specific contexts.

nodes.label: Overdispersion & superspreading
nodes.summary: A small fraction of events can account for many infections, especially indoors with poor ventilation.
nodes.bodyHtml: Risk is uneven: crowded, prolonged, indoor interactions can create large outbreaks even if average risk seems moderate.

nodes.label: Measuring community prevalence
nodes.summary: Case counts, test positivity, wastewater and syndromic surveillance each have biases and delays.
nodes.bodyHtml: Decisions are made with imperfect signals. Under-testing and delayed reporting can hide rapid growth.

nodes.label: Variants, immune escape & changing severity
nodes.summary: Variants can increase transmissibility and partially evade immunity, shifting which mitigations matter most.
nodes.bodyHtml: Controls need to adapt: when transmissibility rises, higher-grade masks and ventilation become more important.

nodes.label: Asymptomatic & presymptomatic spread
nodes.summary: People can transmit without feeling ill, weakening symptom-based controls.
nodes.bodyHtml: If you only send home visibly sick workers, infectious people can still be present—especially early in infection.

nodes.label: Dose, duration & risk
nodes.summary: Risk rises with inhaled dose: time in shared air, proximity, vocalisation, and ventilation.
nodes.bodyHtml: Many mitigations work by reducing dose: fewer contacts, less time, better air, better masks.

nodes.label: Decision-making under uncertainty
nodes.summary: Policy must balance incomplete evidence, delayed outcomes, and competing harms.
nodes.bodyHtml: When evidence is evolving, adaptive policies with clear triggers and feedback can outperform rigid rules.

nodes.label: Public health powers & limits
nodes.summary: Legal authorities for closures, mandates, and quarantine—and the safeguards that maintain legitimacy.
nodes.bodyHtml: Emergency powers must be proportionate, transparent, and time-limited; otherwise trust and compliance decline.

nodes.label: Employer duty of care
nodes.summary: Organisations have obligations to assess risks and implement reasonable controls.
nodes.bodyHtml: A practical duty: identify hazards, consult workers, apply controls, and monitor whether controls actually work.

nodes.label: Worker voice, consultation & whistleblowing
nodes.summary: Channels to raise concerns without retaliation are critical in fast-changing risk environments.
nodes.bodyHtml: Where worker voice is weak, hazards persist and outbreaks can smoulder until they explode.

nodes.label: Supply chain & allocation of scarce protections
nodes.summary: Shortages of PPE/tests create rationing; allocation criteria must reflect reciprocity and risk.
nodes.bodyHtml: Scarcity turns technical choices into ethical decisions: who gets N95s, when, and on what basis?

nodes.label: Data governance: privacy vs protection
nodes.summary: Testing/trace data can protect coworkers but also create stigma and surveillance risk.
nodes.bodyHtml: Good governance clarifies what is collected, who sees it, how long it is kept, and how it is used.

nodes.label: Risk communication & trust
nodes.summary: Clear, consistent communication affects adherence more than rules on paper.
nodes.bodyHtml: Conflicting messages (e.g., 'masks don't work' → 'masks required') can reduce credibility unless uncertainty is explained.

nodes.label: Sick pay & isolation support policy
nodes.summary: Financial support makes isolation feasible, reducing workplace introduction of infection.
nodes.bodyHtml: Without income protection, isolation guidance becomes inequitable and less effective.

nodes.label: Schools, childcare & workforce continuity
nodes.summary: Education policy affects exposure networks and worker availability across sectors.
nodes.bodyHtml: Closing schools reduces contacts for some but increases care burden and can shift risk to carers and informal networks.

nodes.label: Healthcare: patient-facing clinical staff
nodes.summary: High exposure probability: close contact, aerosol-generating procedures, high prevalence among patients.
nodes.bodyHtml: Exposure is shaped by patient volume, ventilation, PPE supply, and training/fit-testing.

nodes.label: Long-term care & care homes
nodes.summary: Concentrated vulnerability: residents at high risk; staff often under-resourced with heavy care demands.
nodes.bodyHtml: Staffing shortages and agency work can connect facilities. Consistent cohorting and paid leave are pivotal.

nodes.label: Public transport workers
nodes.summary: High contact with the public; constrained ventilation and difficulty enforcing masking.
nodes.bodyHtml: Drivers/conductors face repeated short contacts; barriers, air flow, and policy enforcement matter.

nodes.label: Retail & grocery workers
nodes.summary: Continuous public-facing interactions and crowding peaks; limited control over customer behaviour.
nodes.bodyHtml: Queue management, ventilation, and paid sick leave are often more impactful than signage alone.

nodes.label: Hospitality (restaurants, bars)
nodes.summary: High-risk settings when crowded indoors; loud speech and poor ventilation can amplify aerosol load.
nodes.bodyHtml: Mitigation depends on air quality, occupancy, and worker protections during surges.

nodes.label: Education (teachers, support staff)
nodes.summary: Prolonged indoor time with groups; differing risks for teachers vs aides vs custodial staff.
nodes.bodyHtml: Classroom ventilation and masking policies influence risk; inequities appear across job roles.

nodes.label: Warehousing & logistics
nodes.summary: Dense staffing, shared air, fixed quotas; sometimes limited break rooms/ventilation.
nodes.bodyHtml: Operational constraints (speed targets) can undermine distancing, sick leave uptake, and cohorting.

nodes.label: Meatpacking & cold manufacturing lines
nodes.summary: Cold, noisy environments can increase aerosol persistence and vocalisation; workers often face precarity.
nodes.bodyHtml: Crowded lines and shared transport/housing can generate large outbreaks without strong controls.

nodes.label: Gig economy: delivery & rideshare
nodes.summary: Variable contacts; often little access to PPE, sick pay, or clear guidance; risk shifted onto workers.
nodes.bodyHtml: Risk depends on ventilation (car windows), masking norms, and the ability to refuse unsafe jobs.

nodes.label: Cleaning & custodial roles
nodes.summary: Exposure to contaminated spaces and high-traffic areas; often overlooked in PPE allocation.
nodes.bodyHtml: Cleaning staff may enter rooms after infectious occupancy; ventilation time and respirators can matter.

nodes.label: Congregate settings (prisons, shelters)
nodes.summary: High density, limited ability to distance, and constrained healthcare access elevate risk.
nodes.bodyHtml: Outbreak control relies heavily on ventilation, cohorting, testing, and decarceration/alternatives where possible.

nodes.label: Office & knowledge work
nodes.summary: Greater remote-work potential; risk clusters in meetings, shared spaces, and commuting.
nodes.bodyHtml: Hybrid policies, ventilation, and sick leave determine how much risk remains when returning.

nodes.label: Shared indoor air time
nodes.summary: Cumulative time spent breathing the same indoor air as others is a strong driver of risk.
nodes.bodyHtml: This connects occupancy, duration, and ventilation. Reduce shared air time or improve air quality to lower dose.

nodes.label: Crowding & contact network size
nodes.summary: More people in the same space increases both aerosol accumulation and the number of potential sources.
nodes.bodyHtml: Crowding elevates the chance an infectious person is present and increases opportunities for transmission.

nodes.label: Vocalization & emission source strength
nodes.summary: Speaking loudly, shouting, or singing increases aerosol emission.
nodes.bodyHtml: This is why hospitality, gyms, and some manufacturing lines can be disproportionately risky indoors.

nodes.label: Ventilation and fresh-air exchange
nodes.summary: Dilutes and removes infectious aerosols; effectiveness depends on airflow patterns and maintenance.
nodes.bodyHtml: Measure with CO₂ as a proxy for rebreathed air; increase outdoor air and ensure systems perform as intended.

nodes.label: Filtration (MERV/HEPA) & air cleaning
nodes.summary: Removes aerosol particles from recirculated air; works when sized and placed appropriately.
nodes.bodyHtml: Portable HEPA units can materially reduce risk in poorly ventilated rooms when used correctly.

nodes.label: Respiratory protection (mask type & fit)
nodes.summary: High-quality respirators reduce inhaled dose; fit and consistent use determine real performance.
nodes.bodyHtml: Fit-tested respirators outperform loose masks, especially for long indoor exposure.

nodes.label: Testing, screening & rapid isolation
nodes.summary: Detects infectious people earlier, reducing introductions to workplaces.
nodes.bodyHtml: Testing works best with rapid turnaround and support that makes isolation feasible.

nodes.label: Vaccination and hybrid immunity
nodes.summary: Reduces infection risk and substantially reduces severe outcomes, though protection can wane and vary by variant.
nodes.bodyHtml: Vaccination is a key layer but not a standalone solution in high-exposure settings.

nodes.label: Cohorting & network segmentation
nodes.summary: Limits outbreak size by reducing cross-team mixing and repeated exposures across groups.
nodes.bodyHtml: Stable teams and staggered shifts reduce the number of unique contacts, lowering spread potential.

nodes.label: Paid sick leave & isolation support (implementation)
nodes.summary: Operationalising reciprocity: workers must be able to stay home without financial catastrophe.
nodes.bodyHtml: Supports reduce presenteeism, especially in precarious work, and can be among the most effective interventions.

nodes.label: Remote/hybrid work arrangements
nodes.summary: Substitutes physical proximity with virtual work where feasible.
nodes.bodyHtml: Remote work is a strong control when feasible; inequity arises when only higher-paid roles can access it.

nodes.label: PPE allocation & prioritisation rules
nodes.summary: Translates scarcity into practice: who gets higher-grade protection, and when.
nodes.bodyHtml: Allocation should follow exposure risk and reciprocity, and be revisited as supply changes.

nodes.label: Implementation quality & fatigue
nodes.summary: Controls degrade over time: inconsistent use, poor maintenance, and 'pandemic fatigue' reduce effectiveness.
nodes.bodyHtml: A control that exists on paper is not the same as a control that is functioning in practice.

nodes.label: Infection event
nodes.summary: A specific worker becomes infected—linking exposure pathways and controls to an individual outcome.
nodes.bodyHtml: Infections can be mild or severe; even mild cases can cause long COVID or household spread.

nodes.label: Severe disease (hospitalisation/ICU)
nodes.summary: High-consequence outcomes concentrated among older people and those with comorbidities, but not exclusive to them.
nodes.bodyHtml: Vaccination, early treatment, and reduced dose all reduce the probability of severe disease.

nodes.label: Long COVID and chronic impairment
nodes.summary: Persistent symptoms affecting work capacity and quality of life; risk depends on infection, reinfection, and individual factors.
nodes.bodyHtml: Long COVID can disproportionately affect workers with repeated exposures and limited ability to rest or access care.

nodes.label: Mental health burden & moral injury
nodes.summary: Stress, bereavement, burnout, and moral injury, especially in frontline and constrained roles.
nodes.bodyHtml: Repeated high-stakes exposure plus perceived neglect or coercion can cause long-term harm even without infection.

nodes.label: Case: essential worker brings infection home
nodes.summary: A retail worker with no sick pay continues working while mildly ill; household members in crowded housing are infected.
nodes.bodyHtml:
Dilemma: Individual isolation guidance conflicts with financial survival.

Key constraints: precarious work + crowded housing amplify harm beyond the workplace.

nodes.label: Case: PPE scarcity in a hospital surge
nodes.summary: During a surge, staff are asked to reuse respirators; conflicting guidance creates distrust and inconsistent practice.
nodes.bodyHtml:
Dilemma: Maintain care capacity vs protect staff.

Key constraints: supply chain limits, unclear communication, and varying exposure risk across roles.

nodes.label: Case: warehouse speed quotas undermine controls
nodes.summary: Workers skip breaks and work while symptomatic to meet quotas; distancing collapses in break rooms.
nodes.bodyHtml:
Dilemma: Productivity targets vs infection control.

Key constraints: power imbalance, limited leave, and crowded shared spaces.

nodes.label: Case: school with poor ventilation
nodes.summary: Teachers report stale air and repeated outbreaks; upgrades are delayed by procurement and unclear responsibility.
nodes.bodyHtml:
Dilemma: Keep schools open for social benefit vs protect staff/students.

Key constraints: building infrastructure, funding, and governance clarity.

nodes.label: Case: rideshare driver risk transfer
nodes.summary: A driver is penalised for cancelling rides when passengers refuse masks; income depends on acceptance rate.
nodes.bodyHtml:
Dilemma: Enforce safety vs keep income.

Key constraints: platform incentives, weak worker protections, and variable passenger behaviour.

nodes.label: Case: vaccine prioritisation controversy
nodes.summary: A debate emerges: prioritise by age (mortality reduction) vs by occupation (transmission + reciprocity).
nodes.bodyHtml:
Dilemma: Maximise lives saved vs reduce inequity and protect those required to face exposure.

Key constraints: limited supply, public legitimacy, and competing ethical frameworks.

nodes.label: Precautionary principle
nodes.summary: Act to prevent serious harm when evidence is incomplete, while continuously updating actions as evidence improves.
nodes.bodyHtml:
When stakes are high and evidence is evolving, waiting for perfect certainty can cost lives.

Precaution supports early action paired with monitoring and revision.

nodes.label: Stewardship of shared resources
nodes.summary: Manage scarce resources (PPE, tests, hospital beds) responsibly and transparently.
nodes.bodyHtml: Stewardship connects allocation decisions to accountability: who decides, using what criteria, and with what review?

nodes.label: Accountability & auditability
nodes.summary: Decisions should be reviewable; harm should trigger learning and remedy, not denial.
nodes.bodyHtml: Accountability enables course correction and maintains legitimacy when errors occur.

nodes.label: Immigration status & access barriers
nodes.summary: Fear of enforcement, language barriers, and eligibility rules can delay care and testing.
nodes.bodyHtml: Barriers can increase undetected spread and worsen outcomes for marginalised communities.

nodes.label: Disability & chronic illness constraints
nodes.summary: Higher baseline vulnerability and barriers to accommodations can increase harm and reduce agency.
nodes.bodyHtml: Protection measures must include accessible communication, workplace adjustments, and healthcare access.

nodes.label: Community mutual aid & social capital
nodes.summary: Local support networks can buffer isolation and economic shocks, improving feasibility of protective behaviors.
nodes.bodyHtml: Mutual aid can substitute for absent institutional supports, but is unevenly available.

nodes.label: Incubation period & timing of infectiousness
nodes.summary: Timing affects when screening/testing can prevent workplace introduction.
nodes.bodyHtml: Peak infectiousness can occur before symptoms, making rapid testing and masking important.

nodes.label: Reinfection and cumulative risk
nodes.summary: Repeated exposure over waves increases cumulative infection and long-term morbidity risk.
nodes.bodyHtml: Occupations with repeated close contact may face multiple infections over time without sustained controls.

nodes.label: Test sensitivity, timing & false negatives
nodes.summary: Testing can miss early infection; reliance on tests alone can produce false reassurance.
nodes.bodyHtml: Testing works best as part of layered mitigations with clear interpretation rules.

nodes.label: Seasonality & indoor congregation
nodes.summary: Winter indoor crowding and low ventilation can increase transmission.
nodes.bodyHtml: Seasonality shifts the effectiveness needs of ventilation and crowding controls.

nodes.label: Healthcare capacity & system strain
nodes.summary: When hospitals are overloaded, mortality can rise and non-COVID care is delayed.
nodes.bodyHtml: System strain links population-level transmission to individual outcomes beyond infection probability.

nodes.label: Enforcement and inspection capacity
nodes.summary: Rules without enforcement can leave workers unprotected and create uneven compliance across workplaces.
nodes.bodyHtml: Effective governance includes inspection, complaint handling, and credible consequences for neglect.

nodes.label: Procurement & logistics for controls
nodes.summary: Buying and distributing PPE/filters/monitors at scale is a governance challenge.
nodes.bodyHtml: Delays and poor specifications can make technical solutions fail in practice.

nodes.label: Alignment of guidance across agencies
nodes.summary: Conflicting guidance across institutions erodes trust and complicates implementation.
nodes.bodyHtml: Alignment does not require certainty—just transparent explanation of differences and updates.

nodes.label: Workforce planning & surge staffing
nodes.summary: Staffing shortfalls can increase exposure (overtime, burnout) and degrade mitigation adherence.
nodes.bodyHtml: Surge planning includes redeployment training, rest, and protections for high-risk staff.

nodes.label: Compensation, injury recognition & support
nodes.summary: Recognition of occupational COVID and long-term disability affects equity and recovery.
nodes.bodyHtml: Access to compensation can determine whether harmed workers recover financially or fall into poverty.

nodes.label: Home care workers
nodes.summary: Care delivered in private homes: variable environments, limited control of ventilation, high vulnerability of clients.
nodes.bodyHtml: Travel between homes can link households; PPE, scheduling, and support for isolation are critical.

nodes.label: Emergency services (police, fire, paramedics)
nodes.summary: Unpredictable exposures, close contact during emergencies, limited time for controls.
nodes.bodyHtml: Respiratory protection and protocols for confined spaces can reduce risk.

nodes.label: Food processing beyond meatpacking
nodes.summary: Shift work, shared break rooms, and sometimes shared transport; mixed indoor environments.
nodes.bodyHtml: Controls often hinge on break room ventilation, cohorting, and paid leave.

nodes.label: Construction (mixed indoor/outdoor)
nodes.summary: Often outdoors but includes indoor tasks, shared vehicles, and crowded changing areas.
nodes.bodyHtml: Risk can concentrate in vehicles and indoor briefings rather than the outdoor worksite.

nodes.label: Call centers & shared offices
nodes.summary: High vocalization indoors, dense seating, and long shifts; outbreaks observed historically.
nodes.bodyHtml: Air quality and spacing are crucial; masking and filtration can materially reduce risk.

nodes.label: CO₂ monitoring & ventilation management
nodes.summary: Operational feedback tool for air quality and occupancy decisions.
nodes.bodyHtml: CO₂ is a proxy for rebreathed air; targets and actions must be predefined to be useful.

nodes.label: Upper-room UVGI and germicidal UV
nodes.summary: Engineering control that inactivates airborne virus under proper design and safety.
nodes.bodyHtml: UVGI can complement ventilation/filtration in high-risk rooms when designed correctly.

nodes.label: Physical barriers & source shielding
nodes.summary: Screens can reduce short-range droplets but may impede airflow if poorly placed.
nodes.bodyHtml: Barriers should be evaluated for unintended airflow effects; they are not a substitute for ventilation.

nodes.label: Staggered schedules & occupancy reduction
nodes.summary: Administrative control that reduces density and contact network size.
nodes.bodyHtml: Staggering shifts breaks and start times can reduce crowding in entrances and break rooms.

nodes.label: Commute risk controls
nodes.summary: Measures like increased service, masking, and ventilation on transport; employer support for alternatives.
nodes.bodyHtml: Reducing commute crowding can materially lower exposure for workers without remote options.

nodes.label: Hand hygiene & surface cleaning (targeted)
nodes.summary: Important for general infection prevention, though less dominant for SARS‑CoV‑2 compared to airborne controls.
nodes.bodyHtml: Cleaning is most valuable when targeted to shared touchpoints and paired with hand hygiene, not as theater.

nodes.label: Training, fit-testing & donning/doffing
nodes.summary: Quality of PPE use is shaped by training and fit-testing; errors can erase benefits.
nodes.bodyHtml: Fit-testing improves respirator performance; training reduces self-contamination and improves adherence.

nodes.label: Early treatment access and care pathways
nodes.summary: Rapid access to antivirals and clinical care can reduce severe outcomes.
nodes.bodyHtml: Equitable access is critical; delayed care can worsen outcomes, especially in marginalised groups.

nodes.label: Economic harm and insecurity
nodes.summary: Income loss, debt, and job loss can be long-lasting outcomes of isolation, illness, and closures.
nodes.bodyHtml: Economic harm can feed back into risk by reducing the ability to take protective actions.

nodes.label: Educational disruption
nodes.summary: Learning loss and widening gaps due to remote schooling and repeated closures/outbreaks.
nodes.bodyHtml: Educational disruption can have long-term health and economic consequences, and is unevenly distributed.

nodes.label: Case: superspreading event in hospitality
nodes.summary: A crowded indoor venue with loud speech and poor ventilation leads to a large cluster affecting workers and patrons.
nodes.bodyHtml:
Dilemma: Economic survival of venues vs rapid amplification risk.

Key constraints: crowding, vocalization, and low air exchange.

nodes.label: Case: bus driver protection dispute
nodes.summary: Drivers demand barriers and enforcement; policy delays lead to walkouts and disrupted services.
nodes.bodyHtml:
Dilemma: Maintain public services vs protect workers facing daily public exposure.

Key constraints: enforcement feasibility and procurement delays.

nodes.label: Case: care home staffing shortage
nodes.summary: Agency staffing across facilities increases cross-site spread; limiting agency use worsens staffing gaps.
nodes.bodyHtml:
Dilemma: Staffing continuity vs reducing cross-facility transmission.

Key constraints: workforce planning, pay, and employment structures.

nodes.label: Case: punitive use of test data
nodes.summary: Workers avoid testing because positive results trigger discipline or lost shifts, increasing workplace spread.
nodes.bodyHtml:
Dilemma: Use data to protect coworkers vs avoid coercion and stigma.

Key constraints: rights, data governance, and employer incentives.

edges.why: Ethical foundations specify fairness obligations.
Essential work creates reciprocal duties.
Legitimacy depends on proportional response.
Trust requires explainable decisions.
Collective action frames shared responsibility.
Rights constrain what can be collected/enforced.
Equity requires seeing stratified risk patterns.
Privacy/safety trade-offs appear in health data use.
Reciprocity motivates income protection during isolation.
Proportionality guides emergency powers and limits.
Explainability drives effective risk communication.
Solidarity implies duty of care in workplaces.
Lower social position often means more insecure work.
Material conditions influence household transmission.
Transport constraints add exposure outside work.
Discrimination contributes to unequal risk and outcomes.
Insecure platform work limits control and sick leave.
Power imbalance shapes ability to isolate or refuse unsafe work.
Crowded housing turns workplace infection into wider harm.
Remote-capable roles are unevenly distributed.
Childcare constraints shape policy impact across strata.
Airborne spread links to shared indoor air time.
Overdispersion makes crowding and events critical.
Dose increases with time and rebreathed air.
Masks reduce inhaled/emitted dose.
Ventilation reduces airborne concentration.
Testing changes what prevalence signals mean locally.
Variant/immune escape changes vaccine effectiveness for infection.
Higher transmissibility increases value of respirators.
Asymptomatic spread weakens symptom-only screening.
Uncertainty must be communicated to sustain trust.
Duty of care requires ensuring controls work in practice.
Worker feedback detects failing controls and drift.
Scarcity leads to allocation rules for PPE.
Policy becomes effective through operational implementation.
Rules shape how testing data is collected/used.
School policy directly shapes teacher/staff exposure.
Mandates and guidance enable cohorting/occupancy limits.
Clinical exposure often requires respirators.
Surges can create scarcity dilemmas.
Segmentation and stable staffing reduce cross-resident spread.
Passenger density affects contact network and dose.
Many retail workers rely on public transport, compounding risk.
Queueing and peak shopping increase crowding.
Loud speech increases emissions in hospitality venues.
Prolonged shared indoor air drives risk.
Quota pressure can degrade adherence to controls.
Noise drives shouting; cold air may affect persistence.
Masking and ventilation are main personal controls for drivers.
Cleaning roles are often under-prioritised despite exposure.
Congregate density constrains distancing and isolation.
Knowledge work can substitute physical proximity with remote work.
More shared air time increases probability of infection.
Crowding increases likelihood of exposure and transmission.
Higher emission increases dose for others.
Better ventilation reduces infection probability.
Filtration reduces aerosol concentration and risk.
Respirators reduce inhaled dose and infection risk.
Testing reduces introductions by earlier isolation.
Vaccination substantially reduces severe outcomes.
Vaccination can reduce risk of long COVID via fewer/less severe infections.
Poor implementation makes nominal controls ineffective.
Leave support enables staying home, preventing household spread.
Allocation rules determine who faces risk during shortages.
Some infections progress to severe acute disease.
Some infections lead to persistent symptoms and impairment.
Illness, fear, and repeated outbreaks add psychological burden.
Quota-driven workflow can undermine controls.
Poor ventilation and governance delays create repeated outbreaks.
Platform incentives can transfer risk to workers.
Allocation of vaccines creates ethical controversies.
Prioritisation is a policy/legal design choice under scarcity.
Equity frames prioritisation trade-offs.
Reciprocity argues for protecting those required to face exposure.
Precaution informs decisions when evidence is incomplete.
Stewardship frames allocation during scarcity.
Accountability requires mechanisms to detect and correct failures.
Worker voice enables accountability in practice.
Language and trust barriers affect communication reach.
Access barriers can reduce testing uptake and timeliness.
Duty of care includes accommodations and protection for vulnerable staff.
Mutual aid can complement formal isolation support.
Timing determines when tests prevent workplace introduction.
False negatives require layered controls beyond testing.
Repeated infections can increase long-term morbidity burden.
Seasonal indoor congregation increases the importance of ventilation.
System strain can increase severity outcomes and reduce care quality.
Inspections and penalties make duty-of-care requirements meaningful.
Procurement determines whether ventilation upgrades occur on time.
Buying monitors and specifying use supports ventilation management.
Aligned guidance reduces confusion and distrust.
Staffing strategy affects cross-facility movement and cohorting feasibility.
Surge staffing influences exposure, fatigue, and adherence.
Recognition and support affect recovery and financial stability after chronic impairment.
Compensation can mitigate economic harm when illness is occupational.
Home environments vary; shared air time with clients matters.
Home care often depends on worker PPE as primary control.
Unpredictable close contact requires reliable PPE.
Shared spaces and break rooms drive crowding risk.
Shared vehicles and transport can dominate exposure even if work is outdoors.
High vocalization in dense indoor seating increases emissions.
Call-center-like settings can generate large clusters without air controls.
Monitoring provides feedback to manage ventilation and occupancy.
UVGI can reduce airborne transmission in high-risk rooms when designed well.
Barriers target droplets but interact with airflow; must match transmission mechanisms.
Staggering reduces density and contact network size.
Commute controls address exposure added by transport dependence.
Hygiene can reduce some transmission routes; complements airborne controls.
Training and fit-testing improve PPE effectiveness.
Rapid access to treatment reduces progression to severe disease.
Illness and isolation can trigger income loss, especially without support.
School policy and outbreaks drive educational disruption.
Hospitality conditions can produce superspreading events.
Protection disputes can arise where exposure is high and enforcement is hard.
Staffing structures and agency work create cross-facility transmission dilemmas.
Weak safeguards can lead to punitive use of health data.
Fear of retaliation suppresses reporting/testing.
Rights constrain punitive surveillance and discriminatory practices.
Educational disruption often widens inequities.
